coredata.dc:title: A novel bio-based fluorescent N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI composite hydrogel for sensitive detection and efficient capture of toxic heavy metal ions

coredata.dc:description: To address the serious environmental pollution problems of toxic heavy metal ions in water bodies, a novel fluorescent composite hydrogel N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI with a bio-based polymer matrix of carboxylmethyl cellulose (CMC), polyethylenimine (PEI) as a second interpenetrating network and N, P-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) as a fluorescent probe was prepared for simultaneous detection and capture of HMIs by a facile and simple one-step approach. The morphology, chemical structure, swelling ratio, mechanical strength and fluorescence property of these composite hydrogels were studied through varied characterization methods. The composite hydrogel showed sensitive and selective fluorescence response with Hg(II) and Fe(III) and the according LOD values were 0.48 and 0.27 mg L−1, respectively. The relationship between the types of the adsorbent, pH value, HMIs concentration and temperature on the adsorption capacity of these composite hydrogels were studied. The pseudo-second-order model and Langmuir model were applicable to explain the adsorption process of CPH2 for Hg(II) and Cr(VI). The maximum calculated adsorption capacities for the above targeted HMIs by Langmuir model were 846.7 and 289.5 mg g−1, respectively. Coexisting inorganic salts and organic acids in low concentration had little effects on Hg(II) and Cr(VI) removal and the composite hydrogel showed good recyclability and stability for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) removal after four cycles. The electrostatic attraction and coordination covalent bonds were responsible for the adsorption process.

head.ce:title: A novel bio-based fluorescent N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI composite hydrogel for sensitive detection and efficient capture of toxic heavy metal ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the serious environmental pollution problems of toxic heavy metal ions in water bodies, a novel fluorescent composite hydrogel N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI with a bio-based polymer matrix of carboxylmethyl cellulose (CMC), polyethylenimine (PEI) as a second interpenetrating network and N, P-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) as a fluorescent probe was prepared for simultaneous detection and capture of HMIs by a facile and simple one-step approach. The morphology, chemical structure, swelling ratio, mechanical strength and fluorescence property of these composite hydrogels were studied through varied characterization methods. The composite hydrogel showed sensitive and selective fluorescence response with Hg(II) and Fe(III) and the according LOD values were 0.48 and 0.27 mg L−1, respectively. The relationship between the types of the adsorbent, pH value, HMIs concentration and temperature on the adsorption capacity of these composite hydrogels were studied. The pseudo-second-order model and Langmuir model were applicable to explain the adsorption process of CPH2 for Hg(II) and Cr(VI). The maximum calculated adsorption capacities for the above targeted HMIs by Langmuir model were 846.7 and 289.5 mg g−1, respectively. Coexisting inorganic salts and organic acids in low concentration had little effects on Hg(II) and Cr(VI) removal and the composite hydrogel showed good recyclability and stability for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) removal after four cycles. The electrostatic attraction and coordination covalent bonds were responsible for the adsorption process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Many industries including stainless steel production, printing, electroplating, leather tanning and other chemical industries produce large quantities of wastes. The discharge of waste effluents into water resources from these plants contaminates underground water bodies and deteriorates the environment. Among these pollutants, heavy metal ions (HMIs) have drawn serious attentions by researchers ascribed to the non-biodegradable nature and easy bioaccumulation through the food chain and thus destroying the ecology and threating the human health [1,2]. Amongst the various HMIs, divalent mercury ions Hg(II) and hexavalent chromium Cr(VI) are highly toxic, non-essential and non-biodegradable HMIs. Hg(II) with high affinity to thiol groups in proteins can distort the structure, function, and protein transport within cells. Cr(VI) with strong oxidative and migration ability can initiate irreversible respiratory diseases and cause human cell canceration. Therefore, efficient removal of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) from wastewater is of vital concern in environmental fields worldwide [3–6]. Among the various removal methods, adsorption is considered a most practicable method owe to the simple and easy operation, eco-friendly nature, high efficiency, and low-cost. At present, a variety of adsorbents like clay minerals, nanocomposite materials polymers and wastes have been developed to capture HMIs [1–3,7–9].

Recently, the hydrogel, as a new type of adsorbents, has become a hot topic for capture HMIs [10,11]. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), a natural anionic polysaccharide, has been widely used to fabricate bio-based hydrogels owe to the good water solubility, broad resources and low cost [12]. The numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties in the CMC hydrogel facilitate to chelate with HMIs [13–16]. However, the initial CMC hydrogel have the disadvantages of low adsorption capacities for HMIs ascribed to lack of active sites, poor mechanical strength and instability in acidic solutions. A general improved method is to introduce a second component to provide more efficient adsorption sites and mechanic robustness [14]. Li et al. introduced artificial polyacrylamide (PAM) to the CMC hydrogel matrix (CMC/PAM) to give a new bilateral hydrogel for the wastewater remediation [12]. The maximal absorptive capacity of CMC/PAM hydrogel for Cu(II), Cd(II) and Pb(II) were 227.3, 256.4 and 312.5 mg g−1, respectively. Lately, a nanofiller MIL-101(Cr) was added into CMC/PVA hydrogel to improve the adsorption capability and the maximum adsorption for Co(II) and Ni(II) were 180.3 and 261.9 mg g−1, respectively [17]. In addition, polyethyleneimine (PEI) with a plentiful of imine groups has also been introduced to the CMC matrix to enhance HMIs removal [18–22]. Kim et al. reported PEI-functionalized CMC (PEI-CMC) beads with the theoretical maximum Hg(II) adsorption capacity close to 313.1 mg g−1 [21]. Liu et al. prepared a CMC/PEI composite hydrogel with robust mechanical strength and the utmost adsorption ability for toxic Cr(VI) anions was up to 6.01 mmol g−1 [20]. The CMC-based bilateral hydrogel matrix has the following characteristics: (1) A plentiful of hydrophilic functionalities including hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, amide and quaternary ammonium groups on the hydrogel’s surface can highly strengthen the binding effect including electrostatic attraction, chelation and hydrogen bonds. (2) A second network of PEI was interpenetrated with the pristine CMC hydrogel matrix can form a double network hydrogel with robust mechanical strength. (3) The insoluble nature and robust mechanical strength of the bilateral hydrogel make the hydrogel adsorbent easily separated from wastewater after adsorbing HMIs, thus avoiding the secondary contamination.

Carbon dots (CDs), as nano-sized particle, can effectively capture some special HMIs in aqueous solution and act as a fluorescent sensor to detect HMIs [23]. However, the fluorescence property of CDs is relatively poor because of the solid-state aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [24]. To address this issue, various hetero atoms have been doped into CDs for improving their fluorescence properties [25,26]. An obvious fluorescence quenching of heteroatoms-doped-CDs is occurred after contacting with some special HMIs due to the formed complexes between the active groups on the doped-CDs and HMIs [27,28]. Zou et al. reported a cellulose/ N-CDs composite as a fluorescent sensor for Hg(II) detection with the limits of detection (LOD) below 3 nM [29]. CMC hydrogels with CDs enable simultaneous adsorption and detection of HMIs. Guo’s group reported a fluorescent nanocellulosic hydrogel as a adsorbent for capturing toxic Pb(II) with the adsorption capacity up to 2056 mg g−1 and a fluorescent detector for Fe(III) with LOD approaching 18 mg L−1 [30]. Recently, Guo and coworkers developed a fluorescent NCDs-CNF/CS hydrogel for detection and removal of Cu(II) and Cr(VI) ions [11]. The maximum adsorption capacity for Cu(II) and Cr(VI) was 148.30 and 294.46 mg g−1, respectively. The as-prepared hydrogel showed good sensitivity and selectivity towards Cr(VI) with LOD of 0.7093 mg L−1. However, there are rare reports of composite hydrogels with simultaneous removal and sensitive detection of toxic Hg(II) and Cr(VI) from wastewater.

In this paper, a bio-based fluorescent composite hydrogel N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI with CMC and PEI as interpenetrating hydrogel network and nitrogen-, and phosphorus-co-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) as a fluorescent probe was developed for simultaneous capturing and sensitive detection for toxic Hg(II) and Cr(VI) ions. This dual-functional composite hydrogel design was made on the basis of the following considerations: Firstly, the amino-rich PEI and carboxyl-rich CMC hydrogel matrix provide various adsorption sites, which facilitates to give a high adsorption capacity for both positively-charged and negatively-charged toxic HMIs. Secondly, the composite hydrogel was acted as an adsorption-aggregator to enrich the local concentration of HMIs, which result in an obvious fluorescence quenching of N, P-CDs embedded in the hydrogel matrix to realize the sensitive detection. It is expected that these composite hydrogels are capable of simultaneously removal and sensitive detection of toxic HMIs from wastewater. A family of composite hydrogels N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI were fabricated by a facile and simple one-step method. The morphology, chemical structure, swelling ratio, mechanical strength and fluorescence property of N, P-CDs and the composite hydrogels were investigated by varied characterization instruments. The fluorescent responses of N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI for Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions were analyzed. The adsorption isotherms, kinetics, thermodynamics, recycling experiments and adsorption mechanism of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) were also studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and property of CDs and N, P-CDs
characterization of CDs and N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and N, P-CDs were fabricated through a facile solvothermal synthesis strategy. The micro-morphology of the as-prepared CDs and N, P-CDs was measured by HRTEM. From Fig. S1A, the CDs was obviously granular and showed a wide size distribution ranged from 2 ∼ 12 nm with an average size of ∼5.7 nm. However, the N, P-CDs were uniformly and widely dispersed with a narrow size distribution of 3 ∼ 7 nm, and most of these carbon particles had a size distribution of 4 ∼ 6 nm (top inset of Fig. S1B). From HR-TEM images, the crystal lattices of the N, P-CDs was obviously observed (down inset of Fig. S1). The lattice spacing (∼ 0.24 nm) could be assigned to graphite-like carbon with C (100) lattice fringes [31,32].

The FT-IR spectrum was conducted to study the structure of CDs and N, P-CDs. From Fig. S2, a broad peak around 3500–2700 cm−1 was associated the vibrational bands of the O-H and N-H groups. A strong band centered at 1710 cm−1 was corresponded to carbonyl (CO) stretching vibration. Compared with CDs, two distinct characteristics were found of N, P-CDs: (1) Two new adsorption bands at ∼1560 and ∼1400 cm−1 were ascribed to in-plane N-H bending and C-N stretching vibration, respectively. The adsorption band around 1300 cm−1 was mainly induced by P = O stretching vibration.

The X-ray diffraction pattern of CDs and N, P-CDs was presented in Fig. S3. a broad diffraction peak (002) of graphite at 2θ= 23.11° was observed for both samples. Compared with CDs, the absence of the fine peaks of N, P-CDs suggested a non-crystalline structure. Compared with the graphite (0.34 nm) [33,34], the N, P-CDs showed a larger interplay spacing (∼ 0.39 nm), which might be ascribed to the doping process.

From Raman spectrum (Fig. S4), the D peak of N, P-CDs related to the defects and disorder of the sp2–bonded carbon atoms was located at 1463 cm−1, and the according G peak assigned to in-plane vibration of sp2–bonded carbon atoms was at 1528 cm−1. Compared with CDs, the intensity of D and G peaks of N, P-CDs decreased greatly. The degree of the disorder of sp2–bonded carbon atoms could be estimated by the intensity ratio of the D and G bonds (ID/IG). The ID/IG values for CDs and N, P-CDs was calculated to be 1.06 and 0.79, respectively. A larger ID/IG value of N, P-CDs suggested more disorder and defects in the graphitic structure of N, P-CDs.

XPS techniques were chosen to measure the chemical composition of CDs and N, P-CDs. Two distinct peaks near 285 eV and 532 eV indicated that CDs containing C and O elements (Fig. S5A). The C1 s spectrum of CDs was divided into three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.8 eV, which belonged to the characteristic peaks of C-C, CO, and OC-O, respectively (Fig. S5B). The O1 s spectrum of CDs was split into two peaks at 531.9 eV and 533.3 eV due to CO and C-O-C/C-OH, respectively (Fig. S5C). Compared with CDs, a new appearance of N1 s and P2p peaks around 400.0 and 134.0 eV (Fig. S5D), respectively, revealed the presence of N and P elements on the N, P-CDs surface. The N1 s high-resolution spectra had double bonds centered at 399.6 and 401.2 eV, which was assigned to pyrrole-like N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. S5G). As for P2p, double peaks centered at 133.4 and 134.5 eV belonged to P-O and P-C, respectively, was observed (Fig. S5H). These results clearly confirmed a success in doping P and N elements into the skeleton of N, P-CDs. Based on the above analysis, it was inferred that a variety of polar groups like -OH, -NH2, -COOH and -OPO2H were found on the N, P-CDs, which further facilitated to covalently bind with HMIs to realize a sensitive detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and fluorescent property of CDs and N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. S6, it can be seen that CDs had no absorption peak in the range of 250–700 nm. However, The UV-Vis spectra of N, P-CDs showed a strong adsorption band around 325 nm accorded to n-p * transition of the surface chromophores. The PL spectra (Fig. S7) of CDs and N, P-CDs had maximum photoluminescent emitting peaks of 452 and 432 nm when irradiated with 353 and 333 nm UV-light, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs was much larger than that of CDs. The PL decay and the biexponential fitting line were presented in Fig. S8. The calculated fluorescence lifetime of N, P-CDs was 5.3 ns, larger than that of CDs (∼4.2 ns), suggesting doping heteroatoms could improve the fluorescence lifetime. A large Stokes shift, strong fluorescence intensity and long fluorescence lifetime of N, P-CDs indicated a good fluorescence probe for detection of HMIs. Therefore, we chosen N, P-CDs as a fluorescence probe to be further embedded into the hydrogel matrix for further detection of HMIs instead of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI composite hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic procedure and probable structure of these N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI composite hydrogels was shown in Scheme 1. The morphology of the different composite hydrogels from CPH0 to CPH5 were presented in Fig. 1. A typical 3D, sponge-like, and highly microporous network was observed. The pore size of the network was found to be increased with an increase of the PEI content. Taking CPH2 as an example, the pores with the size ranged from nanometers to micrometers in the hydrogel network were irregular. The pore structure of CPH0 and CPH2 was further measured using the common BET method. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm curves and the pore size distribution were presented in Fig. 2 A. Some important parameters obtained from BET results were presented in Tab. S1. The BET surface area of CPH0 and CPH2 was about 5.95 and 2.16 m2 g−1, respectively, larger than that of the conventional ionic resins (<0.1 m2 g−1). Compared with CPH0, the CPH2 sample showed larger pore volume and the average pore width. The according values of CPH2 were 0.024793 cm3/g and 45.9322 nm, respectively. The 3D network and hierarchical pore structures of the composite hydrogels provided massive multi-level HMIs transportation channels, and were conducive to the diffusion of HMIs to the interior of the hydrogel matrix to allow contaminant removal and the local enrichment of HMIs to improve the detection limits.

The FT-IR spectrum of CPH0 and CPH2 were recorded to analyze the structure of these composite hydrogels. From Fig. 2B, a wide band around 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the stacking of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. The adsorption peaks at 1630, 1450 and 1045 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of COO-, C-N and C-O, respectively [35]. Compared with CPH0, the intensity of the weak peaks around 2928 and 2825 cm−1 of CPH2 accorded to C-H antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -CH2- moieties was enhanced due to the numerous -CH2- groups in the PEI. Besides, the typical peaks of epoxy moieties at 1250 and 910 cm−1 was not observed, suggesting a complete chemical reaction between the CMC, PEI and ECH [36,37]. The XRD spectrum of CPH0 and CPH2 were shown in Fig. 2 C. Some typical diffraction peaks of cellulose I crystalline structure at 2θ= 16.8, 21.5 and 34.3° were detected in CPH0, suggesting that the curing process didn’t change the pristine CMC crystal structure [38]. In addition, the CPH2 showed similar diffraction peaks with CPH0, suggesting introducing PEI component has a negligible effect on the pristine CMC crystal structure. XPS analysis was further carried out to test the structure of CPH2. The survey spectrum of CPH2 (Fig. 2D) showed C1 s, N1 s, O1 s and P2p peaks. A high proportion of N elements (12.78 at%) indicated a success in introducing PEI into the CMC hydrogel matrix. The detection of P2p peaks confirmed that N, P-CDs were successfully embedded into the hydrogel structure. The C1 s high-resolution spectra of CPH2 (Fig. 2E) consisted of three peaks at around 284.8, 285.8 and 287.9 eV belonged to C-C, C-N/C-O, and O-CO, respectively. The O1 s high-resolution spectra had two peaks around 531.5 and 532.5 eV, belonged to characteristic peaks of C-O/O-CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2 F). From the N1 s spectrum, the peaks around 401.9, 399.8 and 398.9 eV were accorded to -NH3 +/-NH2 +-, -NH2/-NH- and C-N, respectively (Fig. 2 G). Numerous functionalities rich with heteroatoms (e.g. O, N and P) of the composite hydrogel provided substantial active sites for the adsorption and sensing of HMIs.

The dynamic swelling ratio of the varied composite hydrogels was shown in Fig. S9. The aerogel swelled quickly within the first 60 min due to the presence of various hydrophilic functionalities. The swelling ratio reached an equilibrium after ∼180 min. The CPH3 and CPH5 samples had a maximum swelling ratio (SR) up to 650 %. However, the equilibrium swelling ratio of CPH1 was the lowest amongst all the tested samples. A reasonable explain was that approximate equal positively-charged groups (e.g. RNH3 +) and negatively-charged groups (e.g. -COO-) of CPH1 had strong electrostatic attractions and thus resulted in a poor swelling performance. The high equilibrium swelling ratio was conductive to the mass transfer of HMIs.

Mechanical robustness was a key factor for the hydrogel adsorbent, so the tensile and compressive experiments were tested. Fig. S10A showed the tensile stress/strain curve of CPH3 and CPH5. The yield stress of CPH3 was 45 KPa at the strain of 41 %, while the yield stress of CPH5 was 52 KPa at the strain of 62 %. Besides, the compression modulus of CPH3 was 24 kPa at the strain of 37 %, while the compressive strength of CPH5 was 7 kPa with a strain of 76 % (Fig. S10B). The increased elasticity and toughness of these composite hydrogels was ascribed to the introduction of PEI resulting in an interpenetrating double-network structure with robust mechanical strength.

Thermogravimetric (TG) and DTG analysis of these composite hydrogels were performed to test their thermal stability. From Fig. 2H, the weight loss of these samples was simply categorized into three steps. In the initial stage (25–250 °C), the weight loss was quite low, which mainly caused by the loss of the adsorbed water molecules. In the second step (250–400 °C), a rapid and significant weight loss was observed. From DTG curves, the CPH0 had one degradation peaks at 264 °C (Fig. 2I), which represented the degradation of CMC. However, the other hydrogels showed two degradation peaks. The CPH0.5 sample exhibited a strong degradation peak at 300 °C and a weak shoulder peak around 352 °C assigned to the degradation of CMC and thermal decomposition of PEI in the polymer matrix [39,40], respectively, suggesting an enhanced thermal stability. The degradation peak from CPH0.5 to CPH5 sample was shifted from 352 to 387 °C with increasing content of PEI. A reasonable explain was ECH and N, P-CDs cross-linked with PEI and CMC to give an interpenetrating network with more orderly and stable chemical structure responsible for the improved thermal stability [11]. In the third step (400–500 °C), the little mass loss was ascribed to the further decomposition of residual substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection for toxic Hg(II) and Fe(III) cations
ce:section.ce:para: PL spectra were conducted to investigate the fluorescent property of these composite hydrogels. The strongest fluorescence emission peak of CPH3 sample was located at 440 nm after excitation with 342 nm UV-light ( Fig. 3 A). The maximum emission of CPH3 had a red-shift of ∼10 nm, related to N, P-CDs. A large Stokes shift of N, P-CDs in the hydrogel matrix implied a weak self-absorption phenomenon, indicating a strong anti-interference ability, high accuracy and good fluorescence performance in the detection. To study the fluorescence and selectivity of these composite hydrogels, the fluorescence emission of CPH3 after adding in varied HMIs including Al(III), Fe(III), Ca(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cd(II), Hg(II), Mg(II), Mn(II), Pb(II) and Ba(II) were recorded. From Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of CPH3 decreased after adding in the above-mentioned HIMs. The quenching effect towards Hg(II) and Fe(III) are more prominent than that of the other tested HIMs, indicating the N, P-CDs were highly selective for both Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions.

The fluorescence responses of CPH3 to the two above targeted cations were studied. The images showed the fluorescence emission of CPH3 with different Hg(II) concentrations within the range of 0–200 mg L−1 under UV light irradiation (Fig. 3E). From Fig. 3 C, The fluorescence intensity of CPH3 was continued to decline when the Hg(II) concentration increased. The relationship of the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) as a function of Hg(II) concentration provided a straight line. The linear relationship was described according to the following equation [41].

where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of CPH3 before and after adding in Hg(II), respectively. [Q] and Ksv was the concentration of Hg(II) and quenching constant, respectively.

The coefficient of determination (R2 =0.99874) indicated that the F0/F values was well linear with the Hg(II) concentration. Based on 3σ principle, the LOD value of CPH3 towards Hg(II) ion was calculated to be 0.48 mg L−1. Besides, since the composite hydrogel also showed an sensitive response to Fe(III), the detection behavior of CPH3 for Fe(III) was also performed. The images and the curve showed an obvious gradient fluorescence quenching as the Fe(III) concentration increased (Fig. S11A, C). A linear plot of F0/F vs the Fe(III) concentration ranging from 0–80 mg L−1 was also observed (Fig. S11B). The LOD value of Fe(III) was calculated to be 0.27 mg L−1. It was inferred that these hydrogel systems had a high selectivity and great sensitivity for Hg(II) and Fe(III) within a broad detection range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption studies of Cr(VI) and Hg(II)

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the sensitive detection for Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions, the toxic positively-charged Hg(II) and negatively-charged Cr(VI) were chosen to study the adsorption behavior of these composite hydrogels. The adsorption process of the composite hydrogel in the aqueous solution with Cr(VI) was visualized. A piece of CPH2 (20 mm × 40 mm × 3 mm) was soaked into 75 mL of 200 mg L−1 Cr(VI) aqueous solution. As the adsorption time increased, the yellow color of the solution fade and eventually became colorless, suggesting the toxic Cr(VI) ions were continually adsorbed on the composite hydrogel surface ( Fig. 4 A). The adsorption behavior for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) was further studied by SEM coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The detection of Cr and Hg elements after adsorption indicated Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions were adsorbed on the hydrogel surface, respectively (Fig. S12A-C). In addition, the Cr and Hg elements were widely and uniformly dispersed on the hydrogel surface (Fig. 4B-E), which further confirmed the HIMs were accumulated on the composite hydrogel.

Since the surface charge of the hydrogel and the HMIs species in the tested solution were highly depended on the pH value, the relationship of the adsorbing capacity (qe) vs pH values for the two targeted toxic ions was firstly studied. Fig. 5A showed the qe of CPH2 for Cr(VI) vs the pH values in a range of 1 ∼ 8. The qe of CPH2 towards Cr(VI) showed a first-increase and then-decrease trend with increasing pH value, with a maximum at pH= 2. The adsorption capacity was highly depended on the interfacial interactions between the active sites on the hydrogels and Cr(VI) species. A smaller pH value resulted in more protonated imine groups on the hydrogel’s surface. The increased positive charges provided rich adsorption active sites to remove Cr(VI) anions through electrostatic attraction [42]. When pH< 2, although more positively-charged protonated imine structures were produced, the reduction of negatively-charged HCrO4 - and the formation of molecular state H2CrO4 weakened the electrostatic interactions [42,43], resulting in a low adsorption capacity. When the pH value was between 2 to 6, the Cr(VI) anions mostly existed in the form of HCrO4 - and Cr2O7 2-in the solution. HCrO4 - had high affinity with the adsorbent due to the lower adsorption free energy. The quaternary ammonium groups decreased with increasing pH values, and thus weakened electrostatic attraction with negatively-charged Cr(VI), resulted in a reduced Cr(VI) removal. In near-neutral pH solutions, the Cr(VI) species distribution was composed of HCrO4 -, Cr2O7 2- and CrO4 2- in the solution [8]. When pH= 8, Cr(VI) was almost all in the form of CrO4 2- [44]. The adsorption ability was reduced owe to the higher adsorption free energy of CrO4 2- anions.

To avoid the hydrolysis of Hg(II) to form hydroxides, the adsorption of CPH2 for Hg(II) was explored in the pH range of 2 ∼ 6 (Fig. 5B). When the pH was 2, the positively-charged quaternary ammonium salts kept Hg(II) away from the adsorption sites due to the electrostatic repulsion. When the pH increased to 4, the positively-charged quaternary ammonium salts decreased and the negatively-charged carboxylate group increased. The adsorption of Hg(II) on the hydrogel surface occurred due to the chelation and electrostatic interactions between Hg(II) and hydrogel surface functionalities. With further increasing pH value (pH=6), the main mercury species was changed to neutral Hg(OH)2, accounting for 99.97 % [45]. When pH> 6, the Hg(OH)2, Hg(OH)3 - and/or Hg(OH)4 2- was the major species, which resulted in the adsorption capacity of Hg(II) decreasing. Similar findings were reported by Zhang et al. [45]. Therefore, the following adsorption experiments for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions were conducted at the optimal pH conditions, in which the optimal pH values were 2 and 4, respectively.

The adsorption property of the various composite hydrogels for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) were also studied. The adsorption capacity of CMC, CDs@CMC and CPH0 hydrogels towards Cr(VI) was only 2.7, 2.2 and 2.3 mg g−1, respectively, approximately one order of magnitude lower than that of CPH2 sample with qe up to 111.8 mg g−1 (Fig. 5C). This indicated that the PEI component played a crucial role in adsorbing Cr(VI) anions, which further confirmed electrostatic attractions between the positively-charged protonated quaternary ammonium groups and negatively-charged Cr(VI)were the main driving forces responsible for the Cr(VI) removal. The adsorption capacity of CMC, CDs@CMC and CPH0 hydrogels towards Hg(II) was 311.7, 318.2 and 315.8 mg g−1, respectively, indicating CDs and N, P-CDs embedded in the hydrogel matrix, just acted as a fluorescence probe, had little effect on the adsorption process of Hg(II). The adsorption capacity for Hg(II) ions from CPH0.5 to CPH5 was 439.3, 591.7, 804.1, 682.7, and 632.4 mg g−1, respectively, suggesting CPH2 sample showed the highest adsorption capacity (Fig. 5D). Therefore, both the CMC hydrogel matrix and PEI matrix showed positive effects on improving the adsorption capacity of Hg(II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of contact time and adsorption kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the efficient adsorption capacity of CPH2 for the two targeted toxic HMIs, the CPH2 was used as a representative to perform the subsequent adsorption experiments. The relationship between the adsorption capacity and the immersed time was investigated (Fig. 5E). Taking Hg(II) for an example, a rapid increase of qe was clearly seen within the initial 60 min because a plentiful of vacant adsorption sites were presented on the hydrogel surface. As the adsorption time increased, the growth of the qe values declined because the vacant adsorption sites were gradually occupied. An equilibrium plateau was finally reached after adsorption for 300 min. The adsorption process for Cr(VI) anions showed a similar trend.

The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were chosen to fit the adsorption isotherms. The fitting results of the adsorption kinetics were presented in Fig. 5F, G. Some important parameters from the two kinetic models were presented in Table 1. Generally, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model was mostly applicable to the original adsorption stage, so the obtained correlation coefficient value (R2) was relatively low. As for the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, the value of the R2 for Hg (II) and Cr(VI) ions were 0.9987 and 0.9990 (Table 1), respectively, much closer to 1, indicating the latter pseudo-second-order kinetic model more suitable to describe the two targeted HMIs adsorption behavior. The above analysis indicated a chemical adsorption was responsible for the above adsorption processes [10,46].

To further determine the rate-determining step in the adsorption process, the intra-particle diffusion model was chosen to fit the experimental data. The fitting plots of the adsorption kinetics for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) and the according parameters were summarized in Fig. 5H, I and Tab. S2. The three fitted linear plots didn’t cross the origin point, suggesting that the adsorption process involved three stages and the diffusion within the channel was not the only rate-determining step. In the first stage, the ki for Hg(II) was 74.5721 mg·g−1·min−0.5, much larger than that in the other two stages, indicating a rapid Hg(II) diffusion from the bulk solution to hydrogel/solution interface. The second stage represented a membrane diffusion and the according ki (30.7171 mg·g−1·min−0.5) became smaller, suggested a relatively slow diffusion rate. In the third stage, the ki was decreased to 3.0804 mg·g−1·min−0.5, one order of magnitude less than the value in the first stage, suggesting a rate-limiting step. Therefore, the intra-particle diffusion was not the sole rate-determining step but also affected by the membrane diffusion in the adsorption process [47]. A similar trend was observed for Cr (VI) but the according ki value was even smaller, suggesting a slower diffusion process.

Isothermal adsorption was usually taken to determine the adsorption mechanism. To achieve the maximum adsorbing capacity and adsorption isotherm, the adsorption ability of CPH2 with different initial Cr(VI) and Hg(II) concentrations was performed ( Fig. 6A, D). The qe values increased rapidly as the initial HMIs concentration increased, and eventually reached an equilibrium. Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm models were mostly used to analyze the adsorption equilibrium. The according fitting parameters of the two above models were presented in Table 2. The values of R2 fitted with the Langmuir model for both Cr(VI) (R2 =0.9994) and Hg(II) (R2 =0.99975) were larger than the values from the Freundlich model (R2 =0.9555) and (R2 =0.98735), respectively, indicating a homogeneous monolayer adsorption [48]. According to the Langmuir model, the maximum calculated qe values of CPH2 for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) were as high as 289.5 and 846.7 mg g−1, respectively (Table 2). Notably, A comparison of the adsorption capacity of CPH2 for the removal of Hg(II) with some other CMC-based adsorbents was presented in Table 3. The adsorption capacity in our work were remarkably higher than that of the other adsorbents. The results suggested that these composite hydrogels showed remarkable adsorption ability and had potential applications in capturing toxic HMIs from the practical waste water.

Thermodynamic study and the according parameters were summarized in Fig. 6G, H and Table S3, S4. A negative Gibbs free energy change (ΔG0, −15.9 ∼ −17.1 kJ mol−1) suggested the Cr(VI) adsorption was a spontaneous process. The positive entropy change (∆S0, 59.33 J mol−1 K−1) indicated that randomness at the solid/liquid interface increased during Cr(VI) adsorption. The positive enthalpy change (∆H0, 1.8 kJ mol−1) suggested that the Cr(VI) adsorption on the composite hydrogel was endothermic. This result agreed with the endothermic nature of Chitosan/PEI microspheres for Cr(VI) removal [21]. However, the composite hydrogel showed negative values of ΔG0 and ΔH0 in the Hg(II) adsorption, suggesting that the adsorption was a spontaneous and exothermic process. When the temperature increased, the adsorption efficiency decreased, which indicated the optimum operation temperature should be set at the room temperature. A similar trend in Hg(II) adsorption were reported by Arias [57] and Li et al. [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of coexisting ions and organic acids
ce:section.ce:para: The components in the actual wastewater containing Cr(VI) and Hg(II) were relatively complex, the existence of inorganic salts and organic acids might affect the removal of these HMIs. The effect of different types of coexisting salts on Cr(VI) and Hg(II) adsorption were evaluated. From Fig. 7, the types of the tested anions (e.g. Cl-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-) had little influence on the adsorption capacity of CPH2 for Cr(VI) with the different concentrations in the range from 50 to 2500 mg L−1, indicating little inhibitions (Fig. 7A). As for coexisting cations, the adsorption capacity of CPH2 for Cr(VI) almost showed no change after adding different concentration of the alkaline cations like Na+ and K+ ranged from 50 to 2500 mg L−1. However, the inhibition rates of alkaline earth metal ions Mg2+and Ca2+ were higher than that of alkali metal ions Na+, with inhibition rates of 17.7 % and 12.7 % at a concentration of 2500 mg L−1 (Fig. 7B). As for Hg(II) adsorption, an obvious precipitate was formed when adding a high concentration of the tested anions. So a low concentration of the tested anions (10 mg L−1) was chosen to study the effect on Hg(II) adsorption. The adsorption capacity of CPH2 remained almost unchanged after adding the tested anions at 10 mg L−1 (Fig. 7C), suggesting a negligible inhibition. We also tested the effect of the coexisting cations (e.g. Zn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+ and Pb2+) on Hg(II) adsorption. Clearly, the tested cations showed little effect on Hg(II) adsorption at a concentration of 10 mg L−1. The adsorption capacity decreased after increasing the concentration of the coexisting cations. The competition abilities of the tested cations for the adsorption sites of Hg(II) followed the descending order of Pb2+> Mg2+> Al3+> Cu2+>Zn2+(Fig. 7D).

We chosen some organic acids (citric acid (CA), formic acid (FA), acetic acid (AA) and n-butanoic acid (BA)) commonly used to study the effect of different types of organic acids on Hg(II) and Cr(VI) adsorption. From Fig. S13, the adsorption capacity for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) had almost no change after adding the tested four organic acids with a lower concentration. With increasing the concentration of CA, the adsorption capacity for Cr(VI) remained same and decreased ∼14.4 % for Hg(II), respectively, indicating a little inhibition effect on Hg(II) removal. However, the AA, BA and FA showed an obvious adverse effect on the Cr(VI) and Hg(II) adsorption at higher concentration (Fig. S13), mainly because these organic acids blocked the active sites through electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonds. These competitive species only showed obvious inhibition rate at a very high concentration, even much larger than that in the practical wastewater. The inhibition behavior of these competitive species was almost negligible at a concentration in the practical wastewater, which was conducive to practical application.

The recycling experiments were performed to evaluate the regeneration performance. From Fig. 6I, the qe value of CPH2 for Hg(II) decreased slightly when the number of the cycles increased. In detail, the adsorption efficiency remained at about 75 % after four cycles. A similar trend was observed for Cr(VI) (Fig. S14). The qe value for Cr(VI) was 175.4 mg g−1 at the fourth cycle and the adsorption efficiency remained ∼75 % after four cycles. The slightly decreased adsorption ability was mainly attributed to forming stable complexes and/or metal hydroxides between the terminal functionalities on the composites and HMIs. The formed stable complexes and insoluble metal hydroxide precipitation made it difficult to desorb HMIs completely. Overall, the adsorption ability of the CPH2 sample for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) remained high after four cycles, indicating a good regeneration ability of the prepared composite hydrogel.

Water samples from Yuhe River (Datong, P. R. China)) and industrial waste water (Rock Wool Technology Co., Ltd. P. R. China) were collected to evaluate the adsorption behavior of the composite hydrogel in the real water treatment. However, the content of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) in these waters is minimal, so a weight of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) were added into these water samples to prepare 10 mg L−1 Hg (II) and Cr (VI) solution. From the Fig. S15, the residual concentration of Cr (VI) in the river water and industrial waste water was 1.41, and 1.69 mg L−1, almost the same as that in the deionized water (1.27 mg L−1), indicating little effect on the Cr (VI) adsorption. A similar result was obtained in these waters containing Hg (II). Although the components in the river water and industrial waste water are complex with various ions, organic compounds and microorganism, the composite hydrogels showed effective Hg (II) and Cr (VI) removal in the real industrial wastewater.

FT-IR and XPS analysis of the composite hydrogel after capture of the above two toxic HMIs were performed to clarify the adsorption mechanism. From Fig. S16, after adsorbing Hg(II), the N-H and O-H stretching vibration peaks (∼3420 cm−1) of CPH2 decreased slightly, indicating the hydroxyl and amino groups played major roles in HMIs removal. Meanwhile, the OC-O- stretching vibration at 1630 cm−1 was enhanced and a new adsorption band at 1750 cm−1 was also detected. The change indicated that the carboxyl moieties also participated in adsorbing Hg(II). The peak accorded to C-N stretching vibration became widened and red-shifted from ∼1450 to ∼1383 cm−1 after adsorbing Hg(II), which confirmed that the unprotonated nitrogen-containing groups like primary and secondary amines in the PEI units were involved in the adsorption. The change was probably attributed to a coordination effect between the C-N group and Hg(II). The bonding process might be explained by a soft hard acid-base theory, in which C-N bonds as soft bases and Hg(II) ions as soft acids, making it easy for the two species to bind together [59]. A similar change was observed for the composite hydrogel after adsorbing Cr(VI) anions.

According to XPS spectrum, the detection of the characteristic peaks of Cr2p and Hg4 f of CPH2 after adsorbing Cr(VI) and Hg(II) proved that chromium and mercury were adsorbed onto the composite hydrogel ( Fig. 8A). From the Cr2p high-resolution spectrum, The Cr2p peak was deconvoluted into Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 (Fig. 8B). The two typical peaks around 578.0 and 577.1 eV of Cr2p3/2 was belonged to Cr(III) and Cr(VI), respectively, implied that the adsorbed Cr(VI) on the composite hydrogel were partially reduced [60]. And 51.1 % of the adsorbed Cr(VI) anions were reduced to Cr(III). The three bands from the C1 s spectra located at 287.9, 285.8 and 284.8 eV were belonged to CO, C-O/C-N and C-C, respectively (Fig. 8D). After adsorbing Cr(VI), the adsorption bands of CO and C-O/C-N were slightly shifted to higher binding energies of 288.4 and 286.3 eV, respectively, indicating the O and N atoms got involved in the Cr(VI) removal process. From Fig. 8E, the intensity of the peak around 531.3 eV assigned to C-O/O-CO reduced significantly and the peak area ratio of CO (532.7 eV) and C-O/O-CO (531.3 eV) increased from 1.0 to 3.2, indicating the O elements were involved in the complexing and reducing Cr(VI) anions. According to Fig. 8F, the peaks around 401.9, 399.8 and 398.9 eV were belonged to -NH3 +/-NH2 +-, -NH2/-NH- and C-N [36,61,62], respectively. After capturing of Cr(VI), the peaks of -NH2/-NH- and C-N were shifted to 399.4 and 400.3 eV, respectively, suggested the N elements coordinated with Cr(VI). The peak of -NH3 +/-NH2 +- showed almost no shift but the intensity increased significantly, indicating that the electrostatic attractions between the protonated imine moieties on the hydrogel and Cr(VI) anions played a key role for capture of Cr(VI). The peaks centered at 105.2 and 101.1 eV represented Hg4f5/2 and Hg4f7/2 (Fig. 8C). The change of the C1 s high-resolution spectrum after adsorbing Hg(II) was similar to that after adsorbing Cr(VI), indicating that the O and N elements got involved in the adsorption process as active sites (Fig. 8D). The adsorption peak around 531.5 eV according to O-CO disappeared, suggesting strong electrostatic attractions between the deprotonated carboxyl groups and Hg(II) (Fig. 8E). The N1 s spectrum after capture of Hg(II) was similar to that after adsorbing Cr(VI) anions (Fig. 8F), which indicated that the N elements coordinated with Hg(II) cations. The new peak centered at 406.8 eV was belonged to -NO3, which was the adsorbed counterions on the hydrogel surface.

Based on the analysis above, the following mechanism were proposed to explain the adsorption mechanism of these composite hydrogels for capture of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ( Scheme 2): (1) Under pH= 2 condition, the Cr(VI) anions mostly existed as HCrO4 - form. The negatively-charged HCrO4 - species were prone to be adsorbed on the protonated hydrogel matrix through main electrostatic attractions. A part of the adsorbed HCrO4 - was reduced to Cr(III) due to the existence of hydroxyl and further coordinated with N and O elements to be immobilized in the hydrogel matrix. (2) At pH= 4, the Hg(II) was adsorbed by a large number of carboxyl functionalities on the hydrogel network through electrostatic attractions. The adsorbed Hg(II) was further chelated with the amino groups and oxygen-containing functionalities to be loaded in the hydrogel matrix. In addition, the composite hydrogel was acted as an adsorption-aggregator to enrich the local concentration of HMIs. The non-radiative energy transfer occurred between the N, P-CDs and the adsorbed HMIs, resulting in fluorescence quenching [63]. Therefore, the composite hydrogel provided a superior sensitive and selective detection of HMIs.

A recyclable bio-based fluorescent composite hydrogel N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI with CMC and PEI as hydrogel matrix and N, P-CDs as a fluorescent probe was fabricated by a simple one-step method. The morphology, chemical structure, swelling ratio, mechanical strength and fluorescence of these bio-based composite hydrogels were analyzed. The N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI hydrogel showed an interpenetrating network structure in which bio-derived CMC as a pristine matrix and PEI as a second network crosslinked by ECH. The N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI composite hydrogel emitted blue fluorescence upon UV-light irradiation. The composite hydrogel showed a broad linear range of fluorescence response for Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions. The LOD values of CPH3 for the two targeted cations were 0.48 and 0.27 mg L−1, respectively. The adsorptions of CPH2 for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) were fitted well to the pseudo-second-order model and Langmuir model under the optimal pH values. The maximum calculated adsorption capacities for the two targeted toxic ions by Langmuir model were 289.5 and 846.7 mg g−1, respectively. The competitive inorganic salts and organic acids had little effect on the Cr(VI) and Hg(II) adsorption at low concentration. The adsorption capacity hardly decreased after four cycles. The excellent adsorption capacity was ascribed to a combination of electrostatic attractions and coordination bonds between the heteroatom-containing functionalities on the composite hydrogel surface and HMIs. The composite hydrogel showed effective Cr(VI) and Hg(II) removal in real industrial wastewater. The composite hydrogel has great potential as an effective HMIs capturer and detector for the purification of contaminated water bodies.

The discharge of heavy metal ions (HMIs) into water resources from plants contaminates underground water bodies and deteriorates the environment. HMIs threaten the human health due to the non-biodegradable nature and easy bioaccumulation through the food chain. In this work, a novel bio-based fluorescent N, P-CDs@CMC/PEI composite hydrogel was prepared. The adsorption capacities for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) were 846.7 and 289.5 mg g−1, respectively. The detection limit for Hg(II) was 0.48 mg L−1 in range of 1–200 mg L−1. These composites have a great potential as an effective HMIs capturer and detector for the purification of contaminated water bodies.
